briefs.ny-stlawrence-massena:
Massena checks several boxes cleanly. Land is priced at $17,223/ac across 800 acres, already zoned heavy industrial, which explains the 93 land score. Cooling looks favorable too, with an estimated 8.6°C ambient and 17.3°C wet-bulb, and the risk profile is clean: FEMA flood X, moderate seismic, very low hurricane exposure. Power cost at $34/MWh is attractive given the adjacent NYPA St. Lawrence-FDR hydro asset.

The catch is interconnection. The substation is only 1.2 km out, but the ~772 MW of headroom is an estimate, not confirmed capacity, and the queue wait is roughly 5.7 years, which is long for a GPU timeline. Labor and construction scores at 73 suggest local crew depth may need supplementing. The sales-tax exemption under §1115(a)(37) is also live but under repeal review via S9288, so incentive math could shift before financial close.

Advance it, but confirm interconnection timing and incentive durability before committing capital.
briefs.ny-genesee-stamp: WNY STAMP scores well on the fundamentals that are hard to fix later. Land is priced at $14,538/acre across a 1,250-acre shovel-ready parcel zoned heavy industrial, which should simplify permitting. Risk profile is clean: FEMA flood zone X, low USGS seismic rating, very low hurricane exposure. Cooling conditions look favorable too, with a derived wet-bulb of 16.8°C and 10°C ambient, useful for economizer hours. Connectivity
briefs.ny-oneida-marcy:
Marcy screens well on the fundamentals that are hardest to fix later. Connectivity sits at 97/100 and risk at 97/100, with flood zone X, low seismic exposure, and very low hurricane risk. Cooling looks favorable too, with a 9.3°C ambient and 17°C wet-bulb (derived from NOAA/Open-Meteo), which should help air- and water-side economization. Land is reasonably priced at $15,040/acre across 223 acres, zoned heavy industrial, and the Wolfspeed/Nexus corridor means grid and water infrastructure already anticipates industrial-scale draw.

The soft spots are power and incentives. Power & Interconnection scores only 64/100: the substation is 1.9 km away (measured) with an estimated 678 MW of headroom, but the interconnection queue runs about 5.2 years, which is a long runway for a GPU buildout timeline. Energy cost at $47/MWh is decent, though the queue risk matters more here. Incentives sit at 65/100 partly because the §1115(a)(37) sales-tax exemption, while active now, faces a repeal bill (S
briefs.tx-williamson-taylor:
Taylor Technology Park screens well on the fundamentals. The substation is 1.2 km out with roughly 544 MW of headroom (estimated) and a ~1.9 year interconnection wait, at $35/MWh, which is workable for a phased build. Land is cheap at $19,959/ac across 343 acres, already zoned heavy industrial. Risk scores high: flood zone X, very low seismic, low hurricane exposure. Incentives stack reasonably, with the HB 1223 sales-tax exemption and a Chapter 403/JETI property-value limitation of 50%, and the Samsung fab corridor nearby suggests grid and water investment is already underway locally.

The weak point is cooling and climate, scoring only 36/100. Wet-bulb of 24.3°C and ambient 21.8°C (derived, not directly measured) point to real evaporative cooling limits for parts of the year, which likely pushes designs toward air-cooled or hybrid systems and adds capex. The 544 MW headroom figure is an estimate, so it needs confirmation before sizing critical load. No Opportunity Zone status here, so the upper 75% incentive tier is not available.

Advance it to diligence, with coo
briefs.tx-milam-sandow: Sandow Lakes brings real fundamentals to the table. The 1,200-acre parcel sits on heavy-industrial zoning at $23,916/acre, inside a 33k-acre former Alcoa smelter site that already carries 345kV infrastructure, which should ease permitting and site prep. Risk scores are clean: flood zone X, very low seismic exposure, low hurricane risk. Incentives stack well too, with Opportunity Zone status, the HB 1223 sales-tax exemption, and a JETI property-value limitation that could reach 75% given the OZ overlay. The substation is a measured
briefs.ny-erie-lackawanna: The land fundamentals here are hard to fault. At 346 acres for $11,907/ac and zoned heavy_industrial, this brownfield carries the legacy grid infrastructure from the old Bethlehem Steel works, and the substation sits 0.6 km away, measured. Connectivity (96) and risk (97) scores back this up: flood zone X, low seismic, very low hurricane exposure. Wet-bulb of 17.2°C and ambient 10.4°C su
briefs.ny-onondaga-clay:
White Pine's parcel is large and cheap: 1,400 acres at roughly $15,985/acre, zoned heavy industrial, which gives real room for phased buildouts. Connectivity (94) and land (93) score well, and the risk profile is clean, flood zone X, very low seismic and hurricane exposure. Climate is workable too, with a 17.6°C wet-bulb and 10.2°C ambient supporting efficient cooling most of the year. Proximity to the Micron buildout in Clay suggests grid and construction momentum in the region.

Power is the sticking point. The substation is close, 2.1 km, but headroom is only an estimated 289 MW, and the interconnection queue runs about 5.4 years at $48/MWh. That timeline alone could stall any near-term GPU deployment. Incentives are soft too: the §1115(a)(37) sales-tax exemption applies today but is under legislative review (S9288, Feb 2026), so its persistence shouldn't be assumed. Labor and construction sit at a middling 72.

Advance for diligence on interconnection timing and incentive durability, but don't treat this as sh
briefs.ny-niagara-falls: The land fundamentals here look favorable: 344 acres at $16,860/ac with heavy industrial zoning already in place, and a connectivity score of 92 backed by cooling conditions (89) that include a 17.4°C wet-bulb and 10.4°C ambient, both derived from NOAA/Open-Meteo
briefs.ny-montgomery-amsterdam:
Mohawk Valley checks out well on the fundamentals that are hard to change later. Land is 298 acres at $10,764/acre, zoned industrial, so entitlement friction should be limited. The site sits 1.2 km from the substation (measured), and connectivity, cooling, and risk all score in the 80s or higher, with flood zone X, low seismic exposure, and very low hurricane risk. Wet-bulb of 17.8°C suggests decent air-cooling economics, and the Thruway corridor gives it existing fiber and industrial neighbors.

Power is the soft spot. Interconnection headroom is only an estimated ~296 MW, at a composite score of 49/100, and the queue wait is roughly 5.4 years, which does not fit most GPU deployment timelines without a phased approach or early queue position. Power cost at $48/MWh is reasonable but not cheap for NYISO. The incentive picture adds uncertainty too: the §1115(a)(37) sales-tax exemption currently applies, but S9288 (Feb 2026) proposes repeal, so treat that benefit as under legislative review rather than locked in.

Advance
briefs.ny-rensselaer-greenbush:
Capital District East scores 72/100 overall, carried mainly by connectivity (97) and risk (97). The parcel is 262 acres at $7,766/ac, already zoned industrial, and sits 1.3 km from a substation, close enough that interconnect civil work should be modest. Flood zone X, low seismic exposure, and very low hurricane risk make this one of the cleaner risk profiles we've reviewed. Wet-bulb of 18.1°C and ambient of 10.8°C (derived, not measured) suggest favorable hours for economizer cooling.

Power is the constraint. Interconnection score is 49/100, headroom is estimated at only ~167 MW, and the queue wait runs about 4.7 years at $44/MWh. That timeline alone could rule out near-term GPU deployment. Incentives are shaky too: the §1115(a)(37) sales-tax exemption applies today but is under legislative review via S9288, filed February 2026, so we should not underwrite savings from it without a fallback case. No Opportunity Zone benefit either.

Worth carrying forward for site diligence and a firmer interconnection timeline, but tre
briefs.ny-monroe-rochester:
Eastman Business Park is in Monroe County (NY, NYISO) and scores 72 of 100. Legacy Kodak industrial campus in Rochester with on-site utilities. Its stronger marks are connectivity (99) and risk (97). The closest substation is about 0.9 km out, with a rough 189 MW of headroom, power near $45 per MWh, and an interconnection wait around 4.8 years.

Summer wet-bulb runs about 17°C, which caps how many hours the site can lean on free cooling and shapes its PUE. The softer areas are power & interconnection (49) and incentives (56). FEMA maps it as flood zone X, seismic hazard reads low, and hurricane exposure is very low. NY §1115(a)(37) internet-data-center sales-tax exemption currently applies but faces proposed repeal (S9288, Feb 2026), so treat it as under legislative review.

Read together, this is a power-constrained, cool-climate site. On these numbers it belongs near the top of the diligence list.
briefs.ny-dutchess-poughkeepsie:
Dutchess Tech Campus scores well on the categories that are hard to fix later. Connectivity is 97, risk 97, and flood zone X plus low seismic and very-low hurricane exposure make this one of the cleaner risk profiles we've reviewed. Land is reasonable at $8,570/acre for 209 acres, already zoned industrial, and the IBM legacy gives the area technical labor and existing power infrastructure that a greenfield site wouldn't have.

The catch is power. Power & Interconnection sits at 48, and the ~4.9 year interconnection wait is the real constraint, not the 1.9 km substation distance, which is fine. The ~201 MW headroom is an estimate, not a confirmed allocation, so treat capacity as unconfirmed until NYISO responds. Incentives are also shakier than the 71 score suggests: the sales-tax exemption under §1115(a)(37) faces a repeal bill (S9288) introduced this February, so model both scenarios rather than assuming it holds.

Worth a second look, but only if we can get a firmer interconnection timeline and clarity on the tax exemption before committing capital
briefs.ny-chautauqua-dunkirk:
Dunkirk's fundamentals look decent on paper. The 201-acre parcel is zoned heavy industrial and priced around $14,713/ac, and it sits on a former NRG coal site that retained its transmission interconnection on Lake Erie, a genuine advantage for a repower play. Connectivity (86) and cooling (83) score well, with a 17.4°C wet-bulb and 10.5°C ambient (derived from NOAA/Open-Meteo) suggesting favorable free-cooling hours. Risk scores at 100, with FEMA flood zone X and very low seismic and hurricane exposure.

The power picture is the weak link. Power

NY §1115(a)(37) internet-data-center sales-tax exemption currently applies but faces proposed repeal (S9288, Feb 2026), so treat it as under legislative review.

Read together, this is a power-constrained, cool-climate site. Reasonable to hold on the shortlist while stronger sites get worked.

briefs.tx-mclennan-waco:

briefs.tx-ellis-ennis:
Ennis North scores well on the pieces that usually derail these deals. Flood zone X, very low seismic and hurricane risk, and a 97/100 risk score give real confidence in insurability and uptime. Connectivity at 86/100 and a fiber-friendly I-45 corridor with rail access support both construction logistics and network buildout. Incentives are unusually deep here: Opportunity Zone status stacks with the HB 1223 sales-tax exemption and a JETI/Chapter 403 property-value limitation that could reach 75%. Land is cheap at $15,548/ac across 283 acres, already zoned industrial.

Power is the softer spot. The substation sits 2.8 km out with an estimated 287 MW of headroom, but that figure isn't firm, and the interconnection queue runs about 2.9 years, which will shape any phased buildout. At $40/MWh, energy cost looks reasonable for ERCOT. Cooling is the bigger concern: a 39/100 score reflects a 24.2°C wet-bulb and 20.6°C ambient average, both derived rather than measured, which points to real reliance on mechanical cooling and higher opex than a drier climate site.
briefs.tx-dallas-lancaster: Lancaster's numbers lean positive on the things that are hard to fix later. briefs.tx-johnson-cleburne:
Cleburne screens well on the fundamentals that usually sink these deals. Connectivity is high at 88, and the substation sits 0.5 km out (measured) with roughly 167 MW of headroom, though that figure is an estimate pending a formal study. Power pricing at $36/MWh is reasonable for ERCOT. Risk is clean: FEMA flood zone X, very low seismic and hurricane exposure, 97/100 overall. Land runs 262 acres at $12,769/acre, zoned industrial already, and the state incentive stack (HB 1223 sales-tax exemption, Chapter 403/JETI 50% value limitation) adds real economics even without Opportunity Zone status.

The soft spot is cooling and climate at 42, with wet-bulb near 23.5°C, pushing toward air-cooled or hybrid designs and higher parasitic load. Hurricane exposure is rated moderate and should factor into resiliency planning.

briefs.tx-wise-decatur:

Incentives help too: the HB 1223 sales-tax exemption and a 50% Chapter 403/JETI value limitation are real offsets, even without Opportunity Zone status.

briefs.tx-fortbend-rosenberg: Rosenberg's numbers lean favorable in a few areas. briefs.tx-freestone-fairfield:

briefs.tx-wharton-elcampo:
El Campo Plains has a few things going for it. The substation sits 0.5 km away with roughly 223 MW of headroom (estimated) and power priced at $38/MWh, which is attractive if it holds through the ~2.5 year interconnection queue. Land is cheap at $14,064/acre across 184 acres, and connectivity scores 85/100. Incentives are the real draw: Opportunity Zone status stacks with the HB 1223 sales-tax exemption and a JETI property-value limitation up to 75%, pushing that category to 91/100.

The risks are harder to ignore. Cooling and climate score only 32/100, with a 25.4°C wet-bulb derived from NOAA/Open-Meteo data, meaning evaporative cooling will underperform much of the year. Flood zone AE and moderate hurricane exposure add coastal risk despite low seismic concern. Land is zoned agricultural, so entitlement work and possible rezoning delays are likely. The 2.5-year interconnection wait is long for a market moving quickly.

Advance it as a secondary candidate, contingent on confirming actual MW headroom and running the coo
